title: A Color Engine Built for the Brand, Not the Industry Default
subtitle: Perceptual color extraction from content artwork — enabling dynamic theming that feels warm, meets WCAG contrast, and scales across light and dark modes
team: Design-led initiative with engineering partner, Berlin

hero_image.alt: The Color Lab interface: configuration sliders on the left (light- and dark-mode normalization curves, hue-exclusion gradients) and two phone previews of the same Bibel TV content card on the right — one in dark mode, one in light mode — both themed from the same extracted palette

sections.headline: A Color Engine Built for the Brand, Not the Industry Default
sections.subtext: Every streaming app extracts colors from artwork. Most do it in RGB and default to dark mode. We built a K-means extraction engine in OKLCH perceptual color space — so Bibel TV gets warm, dynamic theming that works in both modes, meets AA/AAA contrast, and feels cohesive with the on-air brand.

images.alt: Config UI showing artwork with K-means extracted colors, contrast ratios, and gradient preview
images.caption: The color extraction config UI: from artwork to theme specification.

sections.quote: You can't just invert dark-mode logic. OKLCH color extraction is a different problem entirely.
sections.context: The core challenge: making dynamic color work from first principles

sections.headline: What the Engine Extracts
sections.content:
From a single content image, the API produces a complete theme specification:

**K-means clusters** — Identifies the most prominent colors through K-means clustering. But prominence alone isn't the goal — the algorithm includes a hue and vibrance filter, so dull, neutral grays are excluded. It finds the colors that actually define the artwork's visual character, not just its pixel distribution.

**Base color** — The primary extracted color, used for the card gradient below and overlapping the image. The engine guarantees this color will produce AA or AAA contrast with the extracted text colors. The card gradient is the visual anchor — it connects the content artwork to the UI.

**Accent color** — Used for the logo overlay and as part of the page background gradient. The page gradient is the base color rendered at 80% opacity over the page background. This creates that warm, connected-to-content feel without overwhelming the visual.

**Two text colors** — Extracted to enable multi-tone text treatments, matching Bibel TV's 2024 on-air brand rebrand which uses two-text-color hierarchy for visual sophistication. We ultimately decided this was too colorful and busy for a denser phone display, but the engine produces them — the design team decides whether to use them.

**Page background gradient** — Computed from the base color at 80% opacity over the page background. Creates depth and warmth without competing with the content artwork.

sections.headline: Why Standard Extraction Doesn't Work
sections.content:
Most extraction tools (imgix, off-the-shelf libraries) work in RGB. They optimize for contrast against dark backgrounds. Flip to light mode, and the same extracted colors washed out text or feel too aggressive. You need perceptual control over lightness and chroma — something RGB and HSL don't give you.

OKLCH is a perceptually uniform color space. You can reason about lightness (L) and chroma (C) in ways that enable precise control over contrast ratios, both light and dark mode themes simultaneously, and gradient blending that feels natural.

sections.headline: Key Design Decisions
sections.content:
**Design-led infrastructure** — I owned the color extraction architecture, not just the Figma design. This is unusual for a Head of Design role. Infrastructure work typically lives with platform engineering. But color extraction *is* a design decision — and making it requires understanding both perceptual color science and the brand's emotional relationship with its users.

**Light mode as default** — The brand identity for Bibel TV is warm, hopeful, light. Users associate the brand with brightness, not darkness. So the extraction pipeline was optimized for light mode correctness first, then verified in dark mode. This is the reverse of every streaming app's standard approach.

**Hue and vibrance filtering** — Not every prominent color is useful. A gray, dark area might dominate pixel count, but it doesn't define the artwork's visual character. The hue and vibrance filter ensures the extraction focuses on colors that create warmth, not neutrality.

**Simplified text colors for phone** — The on-air brand uses two text colors for complex visual hierarchy. But phone displays are dense. Two text colors created visual noise. Sometimes the best design decision is not to use what you built. We kept the engine flexible but shipped with one text color default on mobile.

**Brand consistency through automation** — The 2024 Bibel TV on-air rebrand uses gradients created from a primary brand color overlaid at reduced opacity. The color API replicates this exact approach: base color at 80% opacity, creating a visual language that bridges the app with the broadcast identity.

sections.headline: Technical Architecture
sections.content:
**K-means clustering** — Image converted to pixel array. K-means identifies dominant color clusters. The algorithm works in OKLCH space — perceptually uniform, which means the extracted colors are meaningful to human vision, not just mathematical proximity.

**Hue + vibrance filter** — Clusters below a vibrance threshold are excluded. This prevents neutral grays and desaturated tones from dominating the theme.

**Contrast compliance** — Text colors are validated against extracted base color using the WCAG algorithm. The engine guarantees AA or AAA compliance — no manual checking needed.

**Config UI** — Live administration interface for managing which artwork produces which colors, adjusting extraction parameters, and previewing results. Runs as a web service with access control.

**The API returns** — A complete theme specification: base color, accent, text colors (1-2), page background gradient, logo accent, all validated for contrast compliance and perceptual quality.

steps.label: Image Loading
steps.description: Content artwork loaded from URL or uploaded. Image converted to pixel array.

steps.label: K-means Clustering
steps.description: Algorithm identifies the most prominent colors in the image. Works in OKLCH space for perceptual accuracy.

steps.label: Hue + Vibrance Filtering
steps.description: Low-vibrance clusters filtered out. Only chromatically rich colors pass — the colors that define the artwork's visual character.

steps.label: Role Mapping
steps.description: Clusters mapped to roles: base color (card gradient, highest dominant), accent (logo, secondary), text colors (contrast-optimized for readability).

steps.label: Contrast Validation
steps.description: Text colors validated against base color using WCAG AA/AAA algorithm. If any color pair fails, the algorithm adjusts L and C until compliance is met.

steps.label: Theme Output
steps.description: JSON theme specification returned: all colors in hex/OKLCH, gradient configuration, contrast ratios. Ready for immediate use in app theming.

sections.headline: The Brand Connection
sections.content:
Bibel TV's 2024 on-air rebrand uses a sophisticated color system: primary colors blended at reduced opacity to create gradients, with text in multiple tones for visual hierarchy. The color API was designed to automate this brand language in the digital product.

The card gradient (base color below/overlapping artwork), the page gradient (base color at 80% opacity), the logo accent — these are all digital translations of the on-air visual language. The API ensures that every piece of content artowrk produces a theme that feels connected to the broadcast brand, without manual color selection.

This matters because it keeps the app feeling like Bibel TV — not like Netflix with a different logo. The brand's warmth, optimism, and light are embedded in the extraction algorithm.

metrics.label: Light Mode Preference
metrics.value: 87% of validation survey users preferred light mode (n=40)

metrics.label: Contrast Compliance
metrics.value: All extracted theme colors validated at AA or AAA — zero manual checking

metrics.label: Delivery
metrics.value: API live and running. Config UI accessible. Integrated with Figma plugin for design workflow.

sections.headline: What I Learned
sections.content:
The biggest insight: infrastructure work is design work. The decisions embedded in the color extraction algorithm — hue thresholds, vibrance filters, contrast compliance, perceptual vs. mathematical proximity — are deeply design decisions. When you delegate them to a generic API, you delegate brand quality.

The second: on mobile, restraint beats flexibility. The engine could support complex multi-text looks. But phone displays are dense — the extra text colors created noise, not depth. The API ships with capability it doesn't always use. That's fine. Sometimes the best design is knowing when not to deploy what you built.

The third: the on-air brand system and the digital product should feel like the same brand. Automating the color connection through the API is what makes that possible at scale. Without it, every content piece would need manual color matching — impossible for a content-heavy platform.

sections.headline: Why This Matters for Your Team
sections.content: If your brand has specific visual requirements that industry tools don't match, the answer isn't to compromise the brand. It's to build the tool that does. I designed a color extraction engine because standard tools didn't solve the light-mode problem. Owning that infrastructure — from K-means clustering in OKLCH through contrast compliance through brand-consistent gradient generation — is what makes the final product feel like the brand, not a template.

executive.summary: Built a K-means color extraction API in OKLCH space that powers dynamic theming, card gradients, and WCAG-compliant text for Bibel TV's streaming app. 87% light mode user preference, zero manual contrast checking, brand-consistent automation.
executive.angle: Design-led infrastructure: when industry defaults don't match your brand requirements

blog.summary: Every streaming app defaults to dark mode. We had to make premium work in light — for users who want warmth, not Hollywood. Here's how the color engine works.
blog.angle: The technical depth behind a design problem the industry hasn't solved

meta.source: Bibel TV project interview 2026-05-06 + comprehensive project case study + Figma plugin source inspection
